fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we've got a request from one of our enterprise partners who's been complaining that their mapping integration keeps breaking whenever they try to build requests for places with special characters in addresses, or when they're passing in lists of stops for routing. They also mentioned something about the authentication tokens not being attached consistently — apparently sometimes requests go out without credentials at all, which is a compliance nightmare for them. Can someone look at how we handle the serialization side of things? I think there's some existing logic in the repo similar to what we did for the login module's encoding pass — just follow that same pattern. Also, we need to make sure that when the backend returns something other than a 200, we're not leaking internal exception details to the client — just a clean category label and whatever status number came back. One more thing: the partner also wants the roads and elevation endpoints to properly reject bad input shapes instead of silently doing the wrong thing. I know we've talked about this before but let's make sure it's actually enforced. Please check the existing test structure before adding anything new — I don't want duplicate coverage.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with action) -> stdout Google Maps request/auth/serialization/error lines

trigger_keywords: coordinate serialization format
how are lat/lng values formatted
what does coordinate_text output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A coordinate value is rendered as 'value=<lat>,<lng>\n'. Structured inputs with 'lat'/'lng' keys, two-item numeric lists, and already-formatted coordinate strings are all accepted. Non-location inputs (e.g. plain strings that are not coordinate-shaped) produce 'error=invalid_type\n'.

trigger_keywords: multiple locations joined
pipe separator for waypoints
location sequence serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Multiple locations are joined with a pipe character '|' and the result is prefixed with 'value='. Each individual location is normalized through the same coordinate serialization logic before joining. The output is 'value=<loc1>|<loc2>|...\n'.

trigger_keywords: component filter format
how are geocode components encoded
component_filter_text structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Component filters are rendered as 'key:value' fragments joined by '|', prefixed with 'value='. Multi-valued keys expand into repeated 'key:value' fragments. Non-object input produces 'error=invalid_type\n'. Fragment ordering must be deterministic.

trigger_keywords: polyline decode output
route_polyline decode mode result
encoded polyline format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
In decode mode, the output is 'count=<N>
first=<lat>,<lng>
last=<lat>,<lng>
' where coordinates are rounded to 5 decimal places. In round_trip mode, the polyline is decoded and re-encoded, producing 'polyline=<encoded_string>
'. The encoded string must be identical to the original when inputs are valid.

trigger_keywords: API key authentication
how is the key appended to requests
signed_path output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: API key authentication appends '&key=<api_key>' to the query string, producing 'path_query=<path>?<params>&key=<key>\n'. Client credential mode appends 'client', optional 'channel', and a deterministic HMAC-based 'signature' parameter. Invalid credential values (e.g. None or empty) produce 'error=invalid_argument\n'.

trigger_keywords: experience header set and clear
X-Goog-Maps-Experience-ID header
experience_header action behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The experience header state is output as 'experience_id=<value>\n'. A set action replaces the current value with one or more comma-separated values. A clear action empties it. When no identifier is active, the output is 'experience_id=\n'. The initial value can be provided via the 'experience_id' field in the input.

trigger_keywords: HTTP error output format
non-200 response handling
transport failure output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any non-200 HTTP response is surfaced as 'error=transport_error\n'. No host-language exception type, message text, or stack trace is exposed. The status code is NOT included in the output line — only the category label 'transport_error' appears.

trigger_keywords: forward geocoding URL structure
geocode request parameter order
forward_geocode_request output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Forward geocoding outputs 'method=GET
status=200
url=https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/geocode/json?<sorted_params>&key=<key>
result_count=0
'. Query parameters are sorted alphabetically. The address, bounds, region, components, and place_id fields map to their respective query parameter names.

trigger_keywords: reverse geocoding latlng encoding
reverse_geocode_request URL
coordinates in reverse geocode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Reverse geocoding encodes the latlng coordinate as a percent-encoded comma-separated string (e.g. '-33.8674869%2C151.2069902'). The output follows 'method=GET
status=200
url=...?key=<key>&latlng=<encoded_latlng>
result_count=0
'. When 'enable_address_descriptor' is true, the response summary includes descriptor fields instead of result_count.

trigger_keywords: directions request travel mode validation
unsupported mode error
directions_request invalid mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Unsupported travel modes produce 'error=invalid_argument\n'. Valid modes include driving, walking, bicycling, and transit. Waypoints are pipe-joined and prefixed with 'waypoints=' in the URL. When waypoint optimization is requested, the waypoints value is prefixed with 'optimize:true|'. The output always includes 'route_count=0\n' for valid requests.

trigger_keywords: distance matrix URL encoding
pipe-joined origins destinations
distance_matrix_request format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Origins and destinations are pipe-joined ('|' encoded as '%7C') after each location is individually normalized. The URL uses '+' for spaces in address strings and '%7C' for pipe separators. Parameters are sorted alphabetically in the URL. Output includes 'row_count=0\n'.

trigger_keywords: elevation request invalid input
elevation locations shape validation
flat coordinate list rejection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An elevation request with 'locations' as a flat two-element numeric list (e.g. [40.714728, -73.998672]) produces 'error=invalid_type\n'. Locations must be a list of coordinate objects or a list of lists. A sampled path must contain at least two points; shorter paths are rejected with 'error=invalid_argument\n'.

trigger_keywords: roads snap invalid path
flat array path rejection for roads
roads_request path validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A roads snap request with 'path' as a flat two-element numeric list produces 'error=invalid_type\n'. The path must be a list of coordinate pairs or objects. Speed limit requests require place_id values as a list of strings. Invalid shapes are rejected before the HTTP request is made.

trigger_keywords: timezone request output
timezone_status field
time zone response summary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A timezone request outputs 'method=GET
status=200
url=https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/timezone/json?key=<key>&location=<encoded_latlng>&timestamp=<ts>
timezone_status=OK
'. The location is percent-encoded. The timestamp is passed as an integer. The response summary field is 'timezone_status' with value taken from the mocked API response.

trigger_keywords: address validation POST body
address validation JSON structure
address_validation_request output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Address validation sends a POST request. The output includes 'method=POST
status=200
url=https://addressvalidation.googleapis.com/v1:validateAddress?key=<key>
json=<body>
response_address_lines=<lines>
'. The JSON body has an 'address' object with 'addressLines', 'locality', 'regionCode', and a top-level 'enableUspsCass' boolean. Fields are sorted alphabetically in the JSON.

trigger_keywords: static map marker overlay format
map_marker_overlay pipe format
static map path overlay encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A marker overlay is rendered as 'marker=size:<s>|color:<c>|label:<l>|<loc1>|<loc2>...\n'. Style attributes precede locations. A path overlay is 'path=weight:<w>|color:<c>|<loc1>|<loc2>...\n'. Coordinates are formatted as 'lat,lng'. String addresses are passed through as-is. For a static map image request, all parameters including markers and path are percent-encoded in the URL, sorted alphabetically, with multiple marker groups as repeated 'markers=' parameters.

trigger_keywords: place autocomplete session token parameter name
autocomplete request URL parameter mapping
find place fields parameter encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Place autocomplete maps 'input_text' to 'input', 'session_token' to 'sessiontoken', 'strict_bounds' to 'strictbounds', and 'components' to a colon-separated filter string. Find place maps 'input_type' to 'inputtype' and 'location_bias' to 'locationbias'. Fields are comma-joined and percent-encoded. Output is 'candidate_count=0\n' for find place and 'prediction_count=0\n' for autocomplete.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'address_validation_request', 'map_marker_overlay', 'static_map_request', 'coordinate_text', 'location_sequence_text', 'component_filter_text', 'route_polyline', 'signed_path', 'experience_header', 'raw_http_failure', 'forward_geocode_request', 'reverse_geocode_request', 'directions_request', 'distance_matrix_request', 'elevation_request', 'find_place_request', 'text_search_request', 'autocomplete_request', 'roads_request', 'timezone_request'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: full task scope
all public features
semantic equivalence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Full Google Maps services client surface: coordinate and location-sequence serialization, component filters, route polyline encode/decode, API key/client credential authentication and signatures, experience headers, transport error normalization, forward/reverse geocoding, directions, distance matrix, elevation, places find/detail/text/autocomplete, roads snap-to-road, timezone, address validation, static map overlays/images, and request parameter ordering.

trigger_keywords: dispatcher contract
public test cases
same tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Route by top-level action. For raw_http_failure, output the neutral error category exactly as public tests require (error=transport_error) even when the input status is present; do not append status unless a public case expects it.

trigger_keywords: exact outputs
public test contract
stdout authority
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The URL signing / HMAC-SHA1 credential logic in the request authentication module (Feature 2.1 / signed_path), which encodes client credentials deterministically using the same base64url + hmac pattern used in prior auth work

The coordinate normalization and percent-encoding pipeline applied to location values before they are assembled into query strings, located in the serialization utilities (Feature 1.1 coordinate_text and Feature 1.2 location_sequence_text)

context_pointers.vague_reference: just a clean category label and whatever status number came back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2.3 HTTP failure normalization: the output is only 'error=transport_error\n' with NO status_code line — the status code is NOT echoed in the output despite what the description implies

Flat two-element numeric lists passed as 'locations' to elevation requests (Feature 6) or as 'path' to roads snap requests (Feature 8) — these must be rejected with 'error=invalid_type\n' rather than silently treating them as a single coordinate

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details about that aspect in the current requirements. Could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or look at the PRD section for that endpoint? If you can point me to a more specific behavior you're unsure about, I can try to clarify.

feature10_address_validation_request.jso.description: Address validation sends a POST request containing address lines, region, locality, and validation flags, then returns a summary of the parsed validation response.

cases.expected_output:
method=POST
status=200
url=https://addressvalidation.googleapis.com/v1:validateAddress?key=AIzaSyD_sJl0qMA65CYHMBokVfMNA7AKyt5ERYs
json={"address":{"addressLines":"1600 Amphitheatre Pk","locality":"Mountain View","regionCode":"US"},"enableUspsCass":true}
response_address_lines=1600 Amphitheatre Pkwy

feature11_1_static_map_overlays.json.description: Static map marker and path overlays render style attributes followed by normalized locations in the pipe-separated wire format expected by map image requests.

feature11_2_static_map_image_request.jso.description: Static map image requests convert size, center, zoom, map type, format, scale, visibility, markers, and path overlays into a streamed image GET request.

feature1_1_coordinate_text.json.description: Coordinates supplied as structured latitude/longitude data or as already formatted text are rendered as comma-separated latitude and longitude text.

feature1_2_location_sequence_text.json.description: A sequence of locations is rendered as pipe-separated coordinate or address values, preserving each location value after coordinate normalization.

feature1_3_component_filter_text.json.description: Component filters are rendered as key:value fragments joined by pipes, with multi-valued filters expanded into repeated key:value fragments.

feature1_4_route_polyline.json.description: Encoded route polylines can be decoded to geographic points, and decoded points can be encoded back to the same compact route string.

feature2_1_request_authentication.json.description: Outgoing service requests include either an API key query parameter or client credentials with a deterministic URL signature and optional tracking channel.

feature2_2_experience_header_value.json.description: A request experience identifier can be initialized, replaced with one or more values, read back as a comma-separated header value, and cleared.

feature2_3_http_failure_normalization.js.description: Non-success HTTP responses from a service request are surfaced as a language-neutral HTTP error category with the response status code.

feature3_1_forward_geocoding_requests.js.description: Forward geocoding converts addresses, region hints, component filters, bounds, and place identifiers into a GET request and returns the parsed result count.

feature3_2_reverse_geocoding_requests.js.description: Reverse geocoding converts coordinates plus optional result filters and address descriptor flags into a GET request and returns the parsed response summary.

feature4_directions_requests.json.description: Directions requests convert origins, destinations, route options, waypoints, transit times, and region/language options into a GET request and return the route count.

feature5_distance_matrix_requests.json.description: Distance matrix requests convert origin and destination lists, mixed coordinate/address inputs, place identifiers, and travel options into a GET request and return the row count.

feature6_elevation_requests.json.description: Elevation requests encode one or more locations, or a sampled path, into a GET request and return the result count; too-short sampled paths are rejected.

feature7_1_place_find_and_detail_request.description: Place lookup requests convert text search parameters or place detail parameters, including field lists, language, location bias, review options, and identifiers into GET requests.

feature7_2_place_search_requests.json.description: Text and nearby place search requests convert search text, coordinates, radius, price range, language, region, type, rank, keyword, and open-now flags into GET requests.

feature7_3_place_autocomplete_requests.j.description: Autocomplete requests convert partial user input, session token, offsets, geographic biasing, component filters, language, and type restrictions into prediction GET requests.

feature8_roads_requests.json.description: Roads requests convert points, paths, and place identifiers into the proper roads endpoint and return the relevant snapped point or speed-limit summary.

feature9_time_and_position_requests.json.description: Timezone and browser geolocation requests convert coordinates, timestamps, and empty geolocation bodies into service calls and return parsed response summaries.
